perturbation.original_text: 1. "Proprietary Information" means any information, knowledge, or data that is received by the Receiving Party from the Disclosing Party in furtherance of or pursuant to the Purpose, and that is (a) in writing and clearly marked with a proprietary or confidential legend at the time of disclosure; (b) in a machine - readable form, with the information or the media in which it is provided being clearly marked with a proprietary or confidential legend at the time of disclosure, or if such marking is not practicable, such information or media being identified as proprietary or confidential by written communication of the Disclosing Party prior to or contemporaneously with its disclosure; (c) disclosed orally or visually, with the information being identified as proprietary or confidential at the time of disclosure, and reduced to writing an d clearly marked with a proprietary or confidential legend within thirty (30) days of the initial disclosure; or (d) incorporated or embodied in a sample product or other equipment, material or item clearly marked with a proprietary or confidential legend at the time of disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. "Proprietary Information" means any information, knowledge, or data that is received by the Receiving Party from the Disclosing Party in furtherance of or pursuant to the Purpose, and that is (a) in writing and clearly marked with a proprietary or confidential legend at the time of disclosure; (b) in a machine - readable form, with the information or the media in which it is provided being clearly marked with a proprietary or confidential legend at the time of disclosure; (c) disclosed orally or visually, with the information being identified as proprietary or confidential at the time of disclosure; or (d) incorporated or embodied in a sample product or other equipment, material or item.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement of a proprietary or confidential legend for information incorporated in sample products (section 1(d)) creates a contradiction. Later sections, such as section 6, discuss the handling and return of 'Proprietary Information', implying everything needs to be treated according to initial proprietary designations, but there is no way now to confirm that Proprietary Information is considered that of in 1(d) now that the marking requirement has been removed.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6. All Propr ietary Information shall rema in the property of the Disclosing Party. Upon expiration or termination of this Agreement, unless othe rwise promptly requested by the Disclos ing Party in advance of such expiration or termination, the Receiving Party shall return or destroy (and , in the case of destruction, promptly provide a certification of such destruction to the Disclosing Party) all Proprietary Information in its possession , including , without limitation, any copies, summaries, and/or compilations made of or from Proprietary Information, and shall make no further use of Proprietary Information. Notwithstanding the immediately preceding sentence, the Receiving Party is not obligated to return or destroy (a) any Proprietary Information stored on back -up media for purposes of disaster recovery in the ordinary course of business that may be subject to destruction in due course, pro vided that the Receiving Party is precluded from accessing such Proprietary Information after expiration or termination but prior to its destruction, and (b) residual or latent data such as resulting from deleted files, automatically created temporary files, printer spool files, and metadata that can only be retrieved by computer forensics experts and is generally considered inaccessible without the use of specialized tools and techniques.
perturbation.justification: The original text defined 'Proprietary Information' to include sample products only if they are marked with a proprietary legend. The changed text removes this marking requirement for sample products. Section 6 then states that 'All Proprietary Information shall remain the property of the Disclosing Party' and be returned. Because the marking requirement was removed in the changed text, Section 6 now contradicts the changed definition. Sample products, even without markings, could be considered proprietary, leading to uncertainty about which items fall under Section 6's return obligation. This creates a strong in-text contradiction as it makes the enforcement and understanding of what is considered proprietary information inconsistent.

perturbation.original_text: 5. The Rece iving Party shall permit access to Proprietary Information only to (a) persons who have a need to know for the Purpose and are bona fide employees or contract labor personnel of the Receiving Party ,(b) the Receiving Party's information technology system administrators and service providers in the ordinary course of business , and (c) the Receiving Party 's accountants, attorneys and similar professional advisors : provided that each such recipient is obligated to protect STP NDA rev B Proprietary Information under terms and conditions at least as restrictive as the terms and conditions of this Agreement. Disclosure to any other persons, including consultants , contractors . subsidi aries and affiliates of Receiving Party, shall be treated as disclosure to a third party .
perturbation.changed_text: 5. The Rece iving Party shall permit access to Proprietary Information only to (a) persons who have a need to know for the Purpose and are bona fide employees of the Receiving Party ,(b) the Receiving Party's information technology system administrators and service providers in the ordinary course of business , and (c) the Receiving Party 's accountants, attorneys and similar professional advisors : provided that each such recipient is obligated to protect STP NDA rev B Proprietary Information under terms and conditions at least as restrictive as the terms and conditions of this Agreement. Disclosure to any other persons, including consultants , contractors . subsidi aries and affiliates of Receiving Party, shall be treated as disclosure to a third party .
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'contract labor personnel' from the list of authorized recipients in section 5 creates a contradiction. The text still states that 'Disclosure to any other persons, including consultants, contractors, subsidiaries and affiliates of Receiving Party, shall be treated as disclosure to a third party'. This now contradicts the previous section which implied that contract labor personnel where authorized recipients, which creates uncertainty on how a breach of contract would be treated.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5. The Rece iving Party shall permit access to Proprietary Information only to (a) persons who have a need to know for the Purpose and are bona fide employees of the Receiving Party ,(b) the Receiving Party's information technology system administrators and service providers in the ordinary course of business , and (c) the Receiving Party 's accountants, attorneys and similar professional advisors : provided that each such recipient is obligated to protect STP NDA rev B Proprietary Information under terms and conditions at least as restrictive as the terms and conditions of this Agreement. Disclosure to any other persons, including consultants , contractors . subsidi aries and affiliates of Receiving Party, shall be treated as disclosure to a third party .
perturbation.justification: The original text in section 5 explicitly includes 'contract labor personnel' as authorized recipients of Proprietary Information. The changed text removes 'contract labor personnel' from this list. The subsequent statement in the same section, 'Disclosure to any other persons, including consultants, contractors, subsidiaries and affiliates of Receiving Party, shall be treated as disclosure to a third party,' now contradicts the original intent by potentially classifying contract labor personnel, who were previously authorized, as unauthorized recipients, leading to uncertainty about their treatment and creating an in-text contradiction as per the 'Omissions - In Text Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: 7. The obligati ons under this Agreement as to any Proprietary Information shall continue for twenty (20) years from the date of expiration or termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. The obligati ons under this Agreement as to any Proprietary Information shall continue for twenty (10) years from the date of expiration or termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the duration of obligations from twenty to ten years creates a conflict with section 13, which states 'The Receiving Party's obligations with respect to the protection of Proprietary Information shall survive any termination or expiration of this Agreement for the period of protection specified in paragraph 7 above.' The period of protection is now different, creating ambiguity in the contract on what is the right duration.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 13. This Agreement shall expire five (5) year(s) after its effective date, provided, however, that either Party may terminate the Agreement on an earlier date, without cause, by giving the other Party thirty (30) days written notice. The Receiving Party's obligations with respect to the protection of Proprietary Information shall survive any termination or expiration of this Agreement for the period of protection specified in paragraph 7 above.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies section 7, altering the duration of proprietary information protection from twenty (20) years to ten (10) years. Section 13 explicitly states that 'The Receiving Party's obligations with respect to the protection of Proprietary Information shall survive any termination or expiration of this Agreement for the period of protection specified in paragraph 7 above.' By changing the period in paragraph 7, it creates a direct contradiction regarding the duration of the Receiving Party's obligations after termination, thus creating an in-text contradiction as specified by the provided explanation, as well as the definition of Omissions.

perturbation.original_text: 12. Except for the limited rights expressly granted to the Receiving Party in this Agreement. neither the execution of this Agreement, nor the furnishing of information hereunder, shall be construed as granting to the Receiving Party any right or license under any patent, copyright, trademark , trade secret or other intellectual or industrial property right.
perturbation.changed_text: 12. Except for the limited rights expressly granted to the Receiving Party in this Agreement. neither the execution of this Agreement, nor the furnishing of information hereunder, shall be construed as granting to the Receiving Party any right or license under any patent, copyright, trademark , trade secret or other intellectual or industrial property.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the term 'right' in 'intellectual or industrial property right' in section 12 creates a contradiction. The agreement now specifies property, but not explicitly rights to that property. This introduces conflict because it implies that while the receiving party gains no rights, it is still tied to stipulations of the agreement in regards to property now with undefined rights, since there are no rights being granted. This might be interpreted as conflicting with general standards of property rights.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 12. Except for the limited rights expressly granted to the Receiving Party in this Agreement. neither the execution of this Agreement, nor the furnishing of information hereunder, shall be construed as granting to the Receiving Party any right or license under any patent, copyright, trademark , trade secret or other intellectual or industrial property.
perturbation.justification: The change removes 'right' from 'intellectual or industrial property right' in Section 12. The original text and contradicted text state that the receiving party is not granted any 'right or license' related to intellectual property. By removing 'right' in the changed text, it implies that the receiving party obtains no rights to the property itself, creating uncertainty about property rights and potentially contradicting standard legal interpretations of property ownership elsewhere in the agreement, thus creating a strong contradiction that leads to unenforceability.

perturbation.original_text: 13. The following individuals are designated as the persons to receive Proprie tary Information from the Disclosing Party : For STP Performance Coating, LLC Steve Grass, President; steve@performancecoating.com Todd Didlo, Vice President; todd@performancecoating.com Pete Mathews, Vice President ; pete@ performancecoating.com For Company: Each Party may change its designated recipient by written notice to the other. Receipt of Proprietary Information by an individual other than the designated recipient shall not affect the obligations of the Receiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 13. The following individuals are designated as the persons to receive Proprie tary Information from the Disclosing Party : For STP Performance Coating, LLC Steve Grass, President; steve@performancecoating.com Todd Didlo, Vice President; todd@performancecoating.com Pete Mathews, Vice President ; pete@ performancecoating.com For Company: Each Party may change its designated recipient by written notice to the other. Receipt of Proprietary Information by an individual other than the designated recipient shall not affect the obligations of the Receiving Party if the party informs the original recipient with 30 days notice.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'if the party informs the original recipient with 30 days notice.' introduces an unstated rule for Proprietary Information obligations from third parties. Section 5 directly contradicts that the obligations can only be waived if the party informs the recipient with 30 days notice, which creates conflicting views between Proprietary information obligations and potential recipients.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5. The Rece iving Party shall permit access to Proprietary Information only to (a) persons who have a need to know for the Purpose and are bona fide employees of the Receiving Party ,(b) the Receiving Party's information technology system administrators and service providers in the ordinary course of business , and (c) the Receiving Party 's accountants, attorneys and similar professional advisors : provided that each such recipient is obligated to protect STP NDA rev B Proprietary Information under terms and conditions at least as restrictive as the terms and conditions of this Agreement. Disclosure to any other persons, including consultants , contractors . subsidi aries and affiliates of Receiving Party, shall be treated as disclosure to a third party .
perturbation.justification: The original text in Section 13 states that receiving proprietary information by someone other than the designated recipient does not affect the Receiving Party's obligations. The changed text in Section 13 adds a condition: that the obligations are only unaffected if the party informs the original recipient with 30 days notice. This contradicts Section 5, which states that access to Proprietary Information is permitted only to specific individuals/groups under specific conditions (employees, IT admins, advisors bound by confidentiality). The changed text introduces a new condition for third-party obligations that is not in Section 5, creating a direct conflict and making it unclear whether the 30-day notice is sufficient for compliance with Section 5's requirements for who can receive and be obligated to protect proprietary information.

perturbation.original_text: 15. Without regard to the duration of its other obligations hereunder, the Receiving Party shall control access to, and use of, Proprietary Information and the direct product thereof in accordance with all applicable foreign and U.S. export laws and regulations, including, without limitation , the Arms Export Control Act and the International Traffic in Arms Regulations (ITAR), 22 C.F.R. parts 120 - 130 and the Export Administration Act and the Export Administration Regulations (EAR), 15 C.F.R. parts 730 - 774 (uExport Laws and Regulations") . In connection with and without limiting the general applicability of the foregoing, the Disclosing Party shall, in addition to the marking requirements of paragraph 1, conspicuously mark any Proprietary Information controlled under the Export Laws and Regulations (detailing the nature of control) and the Receiving Party shall not make or permit disclosure of such Proprietary Information or the direct product thereof to any other company, entity, person, or destination unless (a) the Receiving Party has received the Disclosing Party's express written consent to do so and (b) authorization under STP NDA rev B applicable Export Laws and Regulations have been obtained.
perturbation.changed_text: 15. Without regard to the duration of its other obligations hereunder, the Receiving Party shall control access to, and use of, Proprietary Information and the direct product thereof in accordance with all applicable foreign and U.S. export laws and regulations, including, without limitation , the Arms Export Control Act and the International Traffic in Arms Regulations (ITAR), 22 C.F.R. parts 120 - 130 and the Export Administration Act and the Export Administration Regulations (EAR), 15 C.F.R. parts 730 - 774 (uExport Laws and Regulations") . In connection with and without limiting the general applicability of the foregoing, the Disclosing Party shall, in addition to the marking requirements of paragraph 1, conspicuously mark any Proprietary Information controlled under the Export Laws and Regulations (detailing the nature of control) and the Receiving Party shall not make or permit disclosure of such Proprietary Information or the direct product thereof to any other company, entity, person, or destination unless (a) the Receiving Party has received authorization under STP NDA rev B applicable Export Laws and Regulations have been obtained.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'the Disclosing Party's express written consent to do so and (b)' in the export section (15) creates a conflict. The paragraph initially requires written consent from both the disclosing party and regulatory authorization. After the modification, it requires only regulatory authorization, directly conflicting with the implicit control over Proprietary Information granted in Section 2 ('The Receiving Party shall not disclose Proprietary Information to a third party without the Disclosing Party's express written consent')
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2. The Receiving Party shall not disclose Proprietary Information to a third party without the Disclosing Party's express written consent
perturbation.justification: The original text in Section 15 requires both the Disclosing Party's express written consent and regulatory authorization for disclosure of Proprietary Information. The changed text in Section 15 removes the requirement for the Disclosing Party's express written consent, only requiring regulatory authorization. This directly contradicts Section 2, which explicitly states that 'The Receiving Party shall not disclose Proprietary Information to a third party without the Disclosing Party's express written consent.' The removal of this requirement in Section 15 creates a direct conflict with the obligation stated in Section 2, making the contract inconsistent and therefore creating a strong in-text contradiction as per the definition of 'Omissions - In Text Contradiction'.
